title: Hugh Jackman Almost Never Played Wolverine — And 7 Other Things We Bet You Didn't Know
dek: From a Tom Cruise scheduling nightmare to a Tony Award for singing about a queer Australian pop star to nearly hanging up the claws for good, here's the career you didn't know you needed to know about.

items.heading: Wolverine Was Supposed To Belong To A Completely Different Actor
items.text: Before Hugh Jackman ever put on the claws, the role was locked up by Scottish actor Dougray Scott — until Tom Cruise refused to let him leave Mission: Impossible II in time to start filming X-Men. Scott later said the decision was completely out of his hands, and Jackman only got the part because producer Lauren Shuler Donner suggested bringing in the relatively unknown Aussie as a replacement.

images.caption: The last-minute replacement who became Wolverine for 24 years
The actor originally cast as Wolverine before scheduling swallowed his shot

items.heading: He Was A Musical Theater Guy Long Before He Was A Superhero
items.text: Years before claws and adamantium, Jackman was cutting his teeth on stage as Gaston in the Australian production of Beauty and the Beast and as Joe Gillis in Sunset Boulevard. By 1998 he'd landed a Laurence Olivier Award nomination for playing Curly McLain in a West End revival of Oklahoma! — proof this was never a side gig, it was the whole foundation.

images.caption: The song-and-dance man before the superhero movies existed

items.heading: That Tony Award? It Was For Playing A Bisexual Pop Star, Not A Mutant
items.text: In 2004, Jackman won the Tony Award for Best Actor in a Musical for playing Australian songwriter Peter Allen in The Boy from Oz on Broadway, a role that also earned him the Drama Desk Award the same year. The show got mixed reviews overall, but Jackman's performance was singled out as the reason to buy a ticket.

images.caption: Tony winner for The Boy from Oz, 2004

items.heading: The Wolverine Body Was Basically A Second Full-Time Job For Two Decades
items.text: Jackman's transformations evolved with different trainers over the years — for Deadpool & Wolverine he reportedly pushed his intake up to 6,000 calories a day during the bulk phase before cutting down, and back in 2016 he officially joined the '1,000-pound club' after putting up a combined bench, deadlift, and squat total of over 990 pounds in a single day. Somewhere in between all that, he was also documenting 4 a.m. gym sessions and cold-plunge recovery on Instagram under the hashtag #BecomingWolverineAgain.

images.caption: The 2024 comeback that demanded a whole new level of physical prep

items.heading: He's One Award Away From An EGOT — Thanks To A Circus Movie
items.text: Jackman already had the Tony (2004) and an Emmy (for hosting the 2004 Tony Awards) in his pocket when The Greatest Showman soundtrack won Best Compilation Soundtrack for Visual Media at the 2019 Grammys, putting him three-quarters of the way to the EGOT. All he's missing now is the Oscar — and yes, Les Misérables got him agonizingly close.

images.caption: The Grammy-winning soundtrack that put him one trophy from EGOT status

items.heading: His Best Non-Wolverine Performance Might Be A Victorian Magician
items.text: In Christopher Nolan's The Prestige, Jackman played a magician locked in an obsessive rivalry with Christian Bale's character, a role Nolan cast him in specifically because Angier needed "a wonderful understanding of the interaction between a performer and a live audience." Jackman based his performance on a real 1950s magician named Channing Pollock, and the movie remains one of the clearest proofs that Wolverine was never the ceiling of his range.

images.caption: The rivalry-fueled magician drama that showed off a totally different Jackman
His rival magician and co-star in The Prestige

items.heading: He Basically Starved Himself For Les Misérables — On Purpose
items.text: Playing an emaciated ex-convict in Les Misérables meant Jackman spent hours a day in the gym while surviving on almost no carbs, and famously went without water for 36 hours before shooting the opening prison scene to look properly gutted. He called it "almost more physical... than an X-Men movie," and the performance earned him a Golden Globe and his first Oscar nomination.

images.caption: The role that hurt more than playing Wolverine ever did

items.heading: He Actually Meant It When He Said He Was Done With Wolverine — Then He Wasn't
items.text: After Logan, Jackman has admitted he deliberately declared the role finished, saying he "needed to claim it as the end" to protect the story he wanted that movie to tell. But watching the first Deadpool film planted a seed — he pictured a 48 Hrs.-style buddy dynamic with Ryan Reynolds — and he eventually reversed course entirely, admitting, "I've told everyone that I'm done... then I went, 'You know what? I change my mind.'" That change of heart became Deadpool & Wolverine, a billion-dollar hit and the first time Wolverine ever crossed into the MCU.

images.caption: The guy who un-retired the claws — and doesn't sound sorry about it
Proof that 'the end' was negotiable all along
